title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Note



This example has been adapted from the ongoing work on the FIFA World Cup, InsightNet’s ID Cup https://github.com/EpiForeSITE/idcup.
Now, we will build a more complex model using the ModelSEIRMixingQuarantine() model. The new model has the following components:

title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Preparing the environment

library(epiworldR)
library(measles)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)

params <- list(
  city     = "Miami",
  max_pop  = 10000,
  nthreads = 4,
  seed     = 8812,
  ndays    = 60,
  nsims    = 100
)

Pulling information about the city and population from the data files. The mixing matrix is based on the EpiStorm (Litvinova et al. 2025) survey data, which provides age-based contact patterns for various cities. We will use the mixing matrix corresponding to the specified city in params$city.

# Population matrix
census_age <- fread("data/census_age.csv")[city == params$city]
population <- 487014

# Mixing matrix (age-based, from Epistorm survey data)
mixing_matrices <- readRDS("data/mixing_matrix.rds")

The total population in the census data 487014, exceeds the maximum population size (10000). The population will be scaled down to fit within the specified maximum population size.

N <- min(params$max_pop, sum(population))

census_age[, total := ceiling(total / sum(total) * N)]
N <- sum(census_age$total)

We calibrate the contact matrix to a target basic reproduction number of 2.5, consistent with a moderately transmissible respiratory pathogen (e.g., pandemic influenza). The infectious period is set to 7 days and the per-contact transmission probability to 0.1.

calib_scale <- calibrate_mixing_model(
  contact_matrix         = mixing_matrices,
  target_rep_number      = 2,
  infectious_period_days = 7,
  transmission_prob      = 0.1
)

title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Baseline scenario

# Hospitalization rate such that P(hosp) = 20%
# P(hosp) = hosp_r / (hosp_r + rec_r)
#   => hosp_r = P(hosp) * rec_r / (1 - P(hosp))
h_rate <- 0.2 * (1 / 7) / (1 - 0.2)

seir_model <- ModelSEIRMixingQuarantine(
  name                         = "Respiratory pathogen",
  n                            = N,
  prevalence                   = 10 / N,
  transmission_rate            = 0.1,
  incubation_days              = 5,
  recovery_rate                = 1 / 7,
  contact_matrix               = mixing_matrices * calib_scale,
  hospitalization_rate         = h_rate,
  hospitalization_period       = 7,
  # Detection and intervention parameters
  days_undetected              = 3,
  quarantine_period            = 7,
  quarantine_willingness       = 0.8,
  isolation_willingness        = 0.6,
  isolation_period             = 7,
  # Contact tracing parameters
  contact_tracing_success_rate = 0.5,
  contact_tracing_days_prior   = 4
)


# Adding age-group entities to the model
add_entities_from_dataframe(
  model        = seir_model,
  entities     = census_age,
  col_name     = "age_group",
  col_number   = "total",
  as_proportion = FALSE
)

We will use a single initial infectious case seeded randomly in the population.

get_virus(seir_model, 0L) |>
  set_distribution_virus(
    distfun = distribute_virus_randomly(
      prevalence    = 10,
      as_proportion = FALSE
    )
  )

We can now run the simulations.

saver <- make_saver("outbreak_size", "hospitalizations", "transition")

seir_model |>
  run_multiple(
    ndays    = params$ndays,
    nsims    = params$nsims,
    seed     = params$seed,
    saver    = saver,
    nthreads = 1 # params$nthreads
  )

Starting multiple runs (100) using 1 thread(s)
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________
||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||| done.


We can check the results of the simulations by summarizing the model:

summary(seir_model)

________________________________________________________________________________
________________________________________________________________________________
SIMULATION STUDY

Name of the model   : SEIR with Mixing and Quarantine
Population size     : 10009
Agents' data        : (none)
Number of entities  : 17
Days (duration)     : 60 (of 60)
Number of viruses   : 1
Last run elapsed t  : 0.00s
Total elapsed t     : 5.00s (100 runs)
Last run speed      : 10.84 million agents x day / second
Average run speed   : 11.92 million agents x day / second
Rewiring            : off
Last seed used      : 628712762

Global events:
 - Update infected individuals (runs daily)

Virus(es):
 - Respiratory pathogen

Tool(s):
 (none)

Model parameters:
 - Avg. Incubation days         : 5.0000
 - Contact tracing days prior   : 4.0000
 - Contact tracing success rate : 0.5000
 - Days undetected              : 3.0000
 - Hospitalization period       : 7.0000
 - Hospitalization rate         : 0.0357
 - Isolation period             : 7.0000
 - Isolation willingness        : 0.6000
 - Prob. Recovery               : 0.1429
 - Prob. Transmission           : 0.1000
 - Quarantine period            : 7.0000
 - Quarantine willingness       : 0.8000

Distribution of the population at time 60:
  - (0) Susceptible             :  9999 -> 9906
  - (1) Exposed                 :    10 -> 3
  - (2) Infected                :     0 -> 3
  - (3) Isolated                :     0 -> 5
  - (4) Quarantined Susceptible :     0 -> 30
  - (5) Quarantined Exposed     :     0 -> 3
  - (6) Isolated Recovered      :     0 -> 2
  - (7) Hospitalized            :     0 -> 5
  - (8) Recovered               :     0 -> 52

Transition Probabilities:
 - Susceptible              1.00  0.00     -     -  0.00  0.00     -     -     -
 - Exposed                     -  0.78  0.17     -     -  0.05     -     -     -
 - Infected                    -     -  0.73  0.09     -     -  0.02  0.06  0.10
 - Isolated                    -     -  0.09  0.71     -     -  0.13  0.05  0.02
 - Quarantined Susceptible  0.13     -     -     -  0.87     -     -     -     -
 - Quarantined Exposed         -  0.05     -  0.21     -  0.74     -     -     -
 - Isolated Recovered          -     -     -     -     -     -  0.70     -  0.30
 - Hospitalized                -     -     -     -     -     -     -  0.89  0.11
 - Recovered                   -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -  1.00

title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Extracting and plotting the results

ans <- seir_model |>
  run_multiple_get_results(
    freader = data.table::fread
  )

outbreak_size    <- ans$outbreak_size    |> as.data.table()
hospitalizations <- ans$hospitalizations |> as.data.table()
transition       <- ans$transition       |> as.data.table()


Transition

tmat <- get_transition_probability(seir_model) * 0

t_totals <- transition[, .(total = sum(counts)), by = .(from, to)]
tmat[as.matrix(t_totals[, .(from, to)])] <- t_totals$total

draw_mermaid_from_matrix(tmat) |>
  plot()







Outbreak size

ggplot(
  outbreak_size,
  aes(x = date, y = outbreak_size)
) +
  geom_point(
    color = "steelblue",
    alpha = 0.2,
    size  = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = paste("SEIR outbreak size in", params$city),
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Mixing model with census data (%i simulations)",
      params$nsims
    ),
    x = "Day",
    y = "Cases"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









final_outbreak      <- outbreak_size[date == max(date)]
median_outbreak_size <- median(final_outbreak$outbreak_size)

ggplot(final_outbreak, aes(x = outbreak_size)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title    = sprintf(
      "SEIR outbreak size in %s (median: %.0f)",
      params$city,
      median_outbreak_size
    ),
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Mixing model with census data (%i simulations)",
      params$nsims
    ),
    x = "Final outbreak size",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.











Hospitalizations

hosp_daily <- hospitalizations[, .(Total = sum(count)), by = .(sim_num)]

ggplot(hosp_daily, aes(x = Total)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  labs(
    title    = paste(
      "Daily hospitalizations in SEIR outbreak in",
      params$city
    ),
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "SEIR mixing model (%i simulations)",
      params$nsims
    ),
    y = "Frequency",
    x = "Total Hospitalizations"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Intervention: reducing contact rates by 75%
Interventions in epiworldR can be applied in various forms. Besides changing the initial conditions, we can change the situation midway through the simulation using global events. Global events are functions that can arbitrarily modify the model at user-defined points in time. Some of the common applications are:

globalevent_set_params(): Change model parameters (e.g., transmission rate, recovery rate, etc.) at a specific day.
globalevent_tool(): Distribute a tool (e.g., vaccine, antiviral, etc.) to the population at a specific day.
globalevent_fun(): Apply an arbitrary R function to the model at a specific day. This is the most flexible option, as it allows users to implement any intervention that can be coded in R. However, it is also the least efficient option, as it requires running the simulation in a single thread.







WarningThread safety



Unfortunately, the R programming language is not thread safe. In other words, when we use the globalevent_fun() caller, which essentially wraps an R function, we need to run the run_multiple() function using a single thread.


Global events can, as I mentioned, modify arbitrary parts of the program. In this case, we will use a global event to reduce the contact rates by 75% starting from day 40. This is equivalent to reducing the contact matrix by a factor of 4. To do so, we need two steps:

# Step 1: Create a R function that receives a model
reduce_contact_fourth <- function(m) {

  cmat <- epiworldR::get_contact_matrix(m)
  epiworldR::set_contact_matrix(
    m, cmat / 4,
    as_backup = FALSE
  )

}

# Step 2: Register (add) the event to the model
add_globalevent(
  model = seir_model,
  event = globalevent_fun(
    fun  = reduce_contact_fourth,
    name = "Reduce contact",
    day  = 40
    )
  )

The as_backup = FALSE tells epiworldR to automatically restore the original contact matrix when restarting the simulation. Let’s see how the model changes with this new event.

saver2 <- make_saver("outbreak_size")

seir_model |>
  run_multiple(
    ndays    = params$ndays,
    nsims    = params$nsims,
    seed     = params$seed,
    saver    = saver2,
    nthreads = 1 # params$nthreads
  )

Starting multiple runs (100) using 1 thread(s)
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________
||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||| done.

## Extracting
ans2 <- seir_model |>
  run_multiple_get_results(
    freader = data.table::fread
  )

outbreak_size2 <- ans2$outbreak_size |> as.data.table()

Now we can plot the result:

ggplot(
  outbreak_size2,
  aes(x = date, y = outbreak_size)
) +
  geom_point(
    color = "steelblue",
    alpha = 0.2,
    size  = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = paste("SEIR outbreak size in", params$city),
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Mixing model with census data (%i simulations)",
      params$nsims
    ),
    caption = "Includes intervention reducing contact rates",
    x = "Day",
    y = "Cases"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Exercises

Instead of using the glovalevent_fun(), let’s start a vaccination campaign in which we vaccinate 20% of the population on two separate days. Try to implement this intervention.
Using the parameter intervention, see if doubling the quarantine duration from its current value by day 30 has an effect.
(bonus) One advanced feature of epiworldR is the ability to add custom states and functions. Modify the infectious state to transition to recovered and deceased state (new state). Take a look at the model builder documentation.

title: Part 3: Mixing Models
text:
Session info
This document was generated using the {epiworldR} package version 0.15.1.0 on 2026-04-29. We used R version 4.5.3, running on Linux with x86_64 architecture.

title: epiworld Workshop - InsightNet 2026
text:
Code
library(epiworldR)

# Create a model
mymodel <- ModelSEIRCONN(
  name = "Nasty virus", 
  n = 10000, 
  prevalence = .001, 
  contact_rate = 4,
  transmission_rate = .4, 
  incubation_days = 7, 
  recovery_rate = 1/7
) |> verbose_off() 

# Add a second virus
add_virus(
  mymodel, 
  virus(
    name = "Nasty virus 2",
    prob_infecting = .5,
    recovery_rate = .3,
    ), 
  proportion = .001
)

# Rename the model
set_name(mymodel, mname = "SEIR COVID-19 model")

# Run the model
set.seed(8383)
run(mymodel, ndays = 100)

# Plot the model
# - Set graphical parameters to plot in 2x2 grid
op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = par()$mar * c(1, 1, 1/2, 0))
# - Plot
plot(mymodel)
plot_reproductive_number(mymodel)
plot_incidence(mymodel)
plot_generation_time(mymodel)
# - Restore original graphical parameters
par(op)








Figure 1
Welcome to the InsightNet 2026 workshop Simulating Complex Agent-Based Models with {epiworldR}.
The {epiworldR} R package is a wrapper of the C++ library epiworld. It provides a fast, highly customizable framework for building network-based transmission/diffusion agent-based models (ABM).
Key features of {epiworldR} include:
Additionally, while {epiworldR} was built for models of disease transmission, the {epiworld} framework is general enough to build models for many diffusion processes beyond infectious disease.

title: epiworld Workshop - InsightNet 2026
text:
Requirements
You can install the required packages for the session using the following code:
# The measles and epiworldR packages
install.packages(
  c('epiworldR', 'measles'),
  repos = c(
    'https://uofuepibio.r-universe.dev',
    'https://cloud.r-project.org'
))

# Additional packages
install.packages(c("data.table", "ggplot2", "DiagrammeR", "netplot", "igraph"))

title: Measles outbreak simulation in Miami
text:
Note



The original version of this document is part of the ID Cup project and can be found at https://github.com/EpiForeSITE/idcup. This version is adapted for the InsightNet 2026 course and may contain edits for clarity or relevance to the course content.

title: Measles outbreak simulation in Miami
text:
Preparing the environment

library(measles)
library(data.table)

params <- list(
  city = "Miami",
  max_pop = 10000,
  nthreads = 4,
  seed = 8812,
  ndays = 60,
  nsims = 20
  )

Pulling information about the city and population from the data files. The age distribution is county-level; for Miami, the data are from Miami-Dade County. The mixing matrix is based on EpiStorm (Litvinova et al. 2025) survey data, which provides age-based contact patterns. When the data file contains multiple city matrices, we use the matrix corresponding to params$city; otherwise, we use the single matrix included with the workshop materials.

# Population matrix
census_age <- fread("data/census_age.csv")[city == params$city]
population <- fread("data/population.csv")[city == params$city, Population]

# Mixing matrix (age-based, from EpiStorm survey data)
mixing_matrices <- readRDS("data/mixing_matrix.rds")
if (is.list(mixing_matrices) && !is.matrix(mixing_matrices)) {
  if (!params$city %in% names(mixing_matrices)) {
    stop(sprintf("No mixing matrix found for %s.", params$city))
  }
  mixing_matrix <- mixing_matrices[[params$city]]
} else {
  mixing_matrix <- mixing_matrices
}

# Retrieving vaccination rate
vacc_rate <- fread("data/mmr.csv")[city == params$city, vacc_rate] / 100

The total population in the city data, 487014, exceeds the workshop target population size (10000). The age distribution will be scaled down to keep the live example small.

N <- min(params$max_pop, sum(population))

census_age[, total := ceiling(total/sum(total) * N)]
census_age[, vacc_rate := vacc_rate]

N <- sum(census_age$total)


calib_scale <- calibrate_mixing_model(
  contact_matrix = mixing_matrix,
  target_rep_number = 15,
  infectious_period_days = 4,
  transmission_prob = 0.2
  )

title: Measles outbreak simulation in Miami
text:
Baseline scenario

# Finding the hospitalization rate for
# a 10% hospitalization probability:
# P(hosp) = hosp_r / (hosp_r + rec_r)
#   => hosp_r = P(hosp) (hosp_r + rec_r)
#             = P(hosp) * hosp_r + P(hosp) * rec_r
#   => hosp_r = P(hosp) * rec_r / (1 - P(hosp))
h_rate <- 0.1 * (1 / 3) / (1 - 0.1)

measles_model <- ModelMeaslesMixing(
  n                            = N,
  prevalence                   = 1,
  transmission_rate            = 0.2,
  vax_efficacy                 = 0.97,
  contact_matrix               = mixing_matrix * calib_scale,
  hospitalization_rate         = h_rate,
  hospitalization_period       = 7,
  # Intervention parameter
  quarantine_period            = 14,
  quarantine_willingness       = 0.9,
  isolation_willingness        = 0.9,
  isolation_period             = 4,
  # Vaccination parameters (this will be replaced)
  prop_vaccinated              = 0.95,
  # Contact tracing parameters
  days_undetected              = 2,
  contact_tracing_success_rate = 0.8,
  contact_tracing_days_window  = 4,
  rash_period                  = 3
)


# Adding the entities to the model
add_entities_from_dataframe(
  model = measles_model,
  entities = census_age,
  col_name = "age_group",
  col_number = "total",
  as_proportion = FALSE
)

For Miami, the vaccination rate is approximately 88.10%.

# Creating the distribution function
dist_fun <- distribute_tool_to_entities(
  prevalence = census_age$vacc_rate,
  as_proportion = TRUE
)

# Setting the distribution function
measles_model |>
  get_tool(0) |>
  set_distribution_tool(dist_fun)

For the initial cases, we will use 2 active cases and distribute them randomly across the population.

get_virus(measles_model, 0L) |>
  set_distribution_virus(
    distfun = distribute_virus_randomly(
      prevalence = 2,
      as_proportion = FALSE
      )
  )

We can now run the simulations. The workshop defaults use 20 simulations and a maximum population of 10000 so the example can run quickly during a live session. For a fuller analysis, increase params$nsims and params$max_pop.

saver <- make_saver("outbreak_size", "hospitalizations", "transition")

measles_model |>
  run_multiple(
    ndays = params$ndays,
    nsims = params$nsims,
    seed  = params$seed,
    saver = saver,
    nthreads = params$nthreads
  )

Starting multiple runs (20) using 4 thread(s)
_________________________________________________________________________
_________________________________________________________________________
||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||| done.


We can check the results of the simulations by summarizing the model:

summary(measles_model)

________________________________________________________________________________
________________________________________________________________________________
SIMULATION STUDY

Population size     : 10009
Agents' data        : (none)
Number of entities  : 17
Days (duration)     : 60 (of 60)
Number of viruses   : 1
Last run elapsed t  : 10.00ms
Total elapsed t     : 74.00ms (20 runs)
Last run speed      : 58.41 million agents x day / second
Average run speed   : 160.28 million agents x day / second
Rewiring            : off
Last seed used      : 432123442

Global events:
 - Quarantine process (runs daily)

Virus(es):
 - Measles

Tool(s):
 - MMR

Model parameters:
 - (IGNORED) Vax improved recovery : 0.5000
 - Contact tracing days window     : 4.0000
 - Contact tracing success rate    : 0.8000
 - Days undetected                 : 2.0000

 - Incubation period               : 12.0000
 - Isolation period                : 4.0000
 - Isolation willingness           : 0.9000

 - Rash period                     : 3.0000
 - Transmission rate               : 0.2000
 - Vaccination rate                : 0.9500
 - Vax efficacy                    : 0.9700

Distribution of the population at time 60:
  - ( 0) Susceptible             : 10007 -> 9980
  - ( 1) Latent                  :     2 -> 6
  - ( 2) Prodromal               :     0 -> 2
  - ( 3) Rash                    :     0 -> 2
  - ( 4) Isolated                :     0 -> 0
  - ( 5) Isolated Recovered      :     0 -> 2

  - ( 7) Quarantined Susceptible :     0 -> 7
  - ( 8) Quarantined Prodromal   :     0 -> 0
  - ( 9) Quarantined Recovered   :     0 -> 0
  - (10) Hospitalized            :     0 -> 0
  - (11) Recovered               :     0 -> 7

Transition Probabilities:
 - Susceptible              1.00  0.00     -     -     -     -     -  0.00     -     -     -     -
 - Latent                      -  0.85  0.10     -     -     -  0.04     -  0.01     -     -     -
 - Prodromal                   -     -  0.74  0.26     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -
 - Rash                        -     -     -  0.40     -  0.40     -     -     -     -  0.10  0.10
 - Isolated                    -     -     -     -  0.50  0.50     -     -     -     -     -     -
 - Isolated Recovered          -     -     -     -     -  0.91     -     -     -     -     -  0.09
 - Quarantined Latent          -     -  0.04     -     -     -  0.93     -  0.04     -     -     -
 - Quarantined Susceptible  0.03     -     -     -     -     -     -  0.97     -     -     -     -
 - Quarantined Prodromal       -     -     -     -  0.50     -     -     -  0.50     -     -     -
 - Quarantined Recovered       -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -

 - Recovered                   -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -     -  1.00

title: Measles outbreak simulation in Miami
text:
Extracting and plotting the results

# Extracting the results
ans <- measles_model |>
  run_multiple_get_results(
    freader = data.table::fread
  )

# Converting into data.table format for convenience
outbreak_size <- ans$outbreak_size |> as.data.table()
hospitalizations <- ans$hospitalizations |> as.data.table()
transition <- ans$transition |> as.data.table()


Transition

# Preprocessing
transition

      sim_num  date                    from                      to counts
        <int> <int>                  <char>                  <char>  <int>
   1:       1     0             Susceptible             Susceptible  10007
   2:       1     0             Susceptible                  Latent      2
   3:       1     1             Susceptible             Susceptible  10007
   4:       1     1                  Latent                  Latent      1
   5:       1     1                  Latent               Prodromal      1
  ---                                                                     
6965:      20    60      Isolated Recovered               Recovered      2
6966:      20    60      Quarantined Latent      Quarantined Latent      2
6967:      20    60 Quarantined Susceptible Quarantined Susceptible     24
6968:      20    60   Quarantined Prodromal   Quarantined Prodromal      1
6969:      20    60               Recovered               Recovered     14

# To get a starting point
tmat <- get_transition_probability(measles_model) * 0

# Collapsing the data
t_totals <- transition[, .(total = sum(counts)), by = .(from, to)]
tmat[
  as.matrix(t_totals[, .(from, to)])
  ] <- t_totals$total

draw_mermaid_from_matrix(tmat) |> 
  plot()







Outbreak size

# Plotting outbreak size over time
ggplot2::ggplot(
  outbreak_size,
  ggplot2::aes(x = date, y = outbreak_size)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(
    color = "blue",
    alpha = 0.2,
    size = 1.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = paste("Measles outbreak size in", params$city),
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Mixing model with census data (%i simulations)",
      params$nsims
    ),
    x = "Day",
    y = "Cases"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal()









# Aggregating the final day
final_outbreak <- outbreak_size[date == max(date)]
median_outbreak_size <- median(final_outbreak$outbreak_size)

ggplot2::ggplot(final_outbreak, ggplot2::aes(x = outbreak_size)) +
  ggplot2::geom_histogram(fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = sprintf(
      "Measles outbreak size in %s (median: %.2f)",
      params$city,
      median_outbreak_size
    ),
    subtitle = sprintf(
      "Mixing model with census data (%i simulations)",
      params$nsims
    ),
    x = "Outbreak size",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.









We can save the results for further analysis.

fwrite(outbreak_size, sprintf("%s_outbreak_size.csv", params$city))
fwrite(hospitalizations, sprintf("%s_hospitalizations.csv", params$city))

title: Measles outbreak simulation in Miami
text:
Session info
This document was generated using the {measles} R package version 0.3.1.0 and the {epiworldR} package version 0.15.1.0 on 2026-04-29. We used R version 4.5.3, running on Linux with x86_64 architecture.

title: Part 1: Introduction
text: epiworld is an advanced, highly flexible and performant Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) engine with a focus on epidemiological models. The core of the tool has been implemented in C++–available at https://github.com/UofUEpiBio/epiworld–and it has R and Python wrappers.

title: Part 1: Introduction
text:
Architecture
Although epiworld contains a large number of modules and moving parts, the most important ones are the Model, DataBase, Queue, Event, Agent, Virus, Tool, and Entity.





flowchart TD
  subgraph SE[Simulation Engine]
    M[Model]
    D[DataBase]
    Q[Queue]
    E[Events]
  end

  M -->|Reads/Writes| D
  M -->|Optimizes via| Q
  M -->|Triggers| E

  subgraph In[Instances]
    A[Agent]
    V[Virus]
    T[Tool]
    En[Entity]
  end

  A -.->|"Belongs to<br>(optional)"| En
  A -->|Carries| V
  A -->|Uses| T


  M -->|Coordinates| A
  E -->|Modifies| A


 Main components of epiworld and how they are interrelated. 



From the figure, we can highlight a few things. First, all class instances live within the Model class; Agents or Viruses outside of the model have no meaning for epiworld. Second, the DataBase class keeps track of all the relevant information, including daily counts, transmission events, viruses and tools, etc. Third, although Agents can carry a single virus at a time, models can feature multiple viruses simultaneously1, models can feature multiple viruses in the same run, e.g., multiple strands of COVID-19. Fourth, agents can feature multiple Tools: a class of object that alters agents’ susceptibility, infectiousness, and recovery, e.g., a vaccine.
The library features two types of contact dynamics: network-based interaction and mixing models. In the network based interaction–the fastest models in epiworld–transmission takes place through a pre-defined network. In the case of mixing models, agents are organized in Entity(ies) which determines group-level interaction. Mixing models bridge ABMs with compartmental models, providing an approximation that does not require specifying a network structure.






TipNetwork vs Mixing models



Although network-based models are faster, they usually assume a known contact network which may not be available. Mixing models provide a nice approximation that essentially integrates-out the uncertainty about network-based models. In principle, the same could be done with network-based models, but, in order to do so, we would need to embed the graph-generating algorithm in the model, which could increase the computational complexity.

title: Part 1: Introduction
text:
Dynamics
epiworld is a discrete-time ABM, meaning that, instead of handling a queue of updates and events, it collects updates from a single step and applies them at the end of it. For the user, this simply means that changes in the system that were triggered in time tt will be reflected in time t+1t + 1. Each run of epiworld is characterized by the following sequence:

Reset the model to its initial state: clear the database, reset the agents, and reset the entities.
Distribute agents to entities, viruses to agents, and tools to agents. Each call finalizes with run_events().







NoteLocking changes



The functions set_virus(), add_tool(), add_entity() or update_state() are not effective until run_events() is called. Since epiworld is discrete-time, changes must be applied simultaneously.



Main loop. For each day in 1 ... ndays do:

Queue: Check if agents are in the queue. If not, skip to the next iteration.
Update AgentsIf the agent is in the queue, trigger the update function associated to the agent, e.g., update_susceptible() if the agent is susceptible, update_infected() if the agent is infected, etc.
Global Events: Iterate FIFO through global events. This could trigger more updates to agents, viruses, tools, and entities. For example, a global event could be a lockdown that reduces the contact rate of all agents by 50% for 14 days.
Save changes: Save the state of the model in the database and increment the day counter.

Return: The simulation ends and the user can retrieve information from it.

Because epiworld has been designed to be fast, we generally run multiple simulations, generating a distribution of outcomes instead of single point estimates.

title: Part 1: Introduction
text:
Footnotes


At some point in time, epiworld supported agents being infected with multiple viruses at once. Nonetheless, although an interesting feature, it was computationally expensive. Thus, we decided to simplify the tool and feature a single virus per agent.↩︎

title: Building models with the model builder
text:
Note



The original document is available as a vignette in the epiworldR package. This version is adapted for the InsightNet 2026 course and may contain edits for clarity or relevance to the course content.

title: Building models with the model builder
text:
Introduction
The model_builder functions in epiworldR let you assemble a model one piece at a time. Instead of starting from a predefined model such as ModelSIR() or ModelSEIRD(), you begin with an empty model and add the ingredients yourself. This is useful when you want to understand how a model is constructed or when you need a compartment structure that is not already available in the package.
There are four moving parts:

Parameters, which store values such as transmission or recovery rates.
States, which define the compartments of the model.
Update functions, which tell agents what can happen while they are in a given state.
Viruses, which connect the infection process to the model states.

One important detail is that states are indexed in the order they are added, starting at 0. We will use those indices when defining transitions.

title: Building models with the model builder
section: A minimal model built from scratch
text:
A minimal model built from scratch
We begin with a simple three-state model: susceptible, infected, and recovered. The goal here is to make the model builder workflow explicit before moving to a slightly richer example.

library(epiworldR)

Thank you for using epiworldR! Please consider citing it in your work.
You can find the citation information by running
  citation("epiworldR")

sir_builder <- Model()

add_param(sir_builder, "Transmission rate", 0.01)
add_param(sir_builder, "Recovery rate", 1 / 7)

At this point the model has parameters, but no states. Next we create the update functions. Susceptible agents use update_fun_susceptible(), which evaluates infection from their contacts. Infected agents use update_fun_rate(), which moves them to another state according to a daily transition probability.

update_s <- update_fun_susceptible()
update_i <- update_fun_rate(
  param_names = "Recovery rate",
  target_states = 2L
)

Now we add the states. Because states are indexed from 0, the order matters (we can use the |> operator to make it clear which state gets which index):

sir_builder |>
  add_state("Susceptible", update_s) |>
  add_state("Infected", update_i) |>
  add_state("Recovered", NULL)

data.frame(
  index = 0:(length(get_states(sir_builder)) - 1),
  state = get_states(sir_builder)
)

  index       state
1     0 Susceptible
2     1    Infected
3     2   Recovered


The infected state points to 2L, meaning that infected agents move to the third state added to the model, "Recovered". The "Recovered" state has no update function, so agents who enter that state remain there until the end of the simulation.
The last piece is the virus. Here we define how the virus maps to the model states:

newly infected agents move to state 1L ("Infected"),
agents who recover end in state 2L ("Recovered"),
and agents removed from the virus also end in state 2L.


flu <- virus(
  name = "Flu",
  prevalence = 0.01,
  as_proportion = TRUE,
  prob_infecting = 0.01,
  recovery_rate = 1 / 7
) |>
  # Associating the virus with the model states
  virus_set_state(init = 1L, end = 2L, removed = 2L) |>
  # Linking the virus rate to a model parameter
  set_prob_infecting_ptr(sir_builder, "Transmission rate") |>
  # Setting the distribution (random to 1% of the agents)
  set_distribution_virus(distribute_virus_randomly(0.01, TRUE))

add_virus(sir_builder, flu)

With states and virus in place, we still need a population. The following code creates a small-world network, runs the simulation, and shows the result:

agents_smallworld(sir_builder, n = 5000, k = 10, d = FALSE, p = 0.01)
verbose_off(sir_builder)

run(sir_builder, ndays = 100, seed = 1912)
summary(sir_builder)

________________________________________________________________________________
________________________________________________________________________________
SIMULATION STUDY

Name of the model   : (none)
Population size     : 5000
Agents' data        : (none)
Number of entities  : 0
Days (duration)     : 100 (of 100)
Number of viruses   : 1
Last run elapsed t  : 19.00ms
Last run speed      : 26.16 million agents x day / second
Rewiring            : off
Last seed used      : 1912

Global events:
 (none)

Virus(es):
 - Flu

Tool(s):
 (none)

Model parameters:
 - Recovery rate     : 0.1429
 - Transmission rate : 0.0100

Distribution of the population at time 100:
  - (0) Susceptible : 4950 -> 3186
  - (1) Infected    :   50 -> 153
  - (2) Recovered   :    0 -> 1661

Transition Probabilities:
 - Susceptible  1.00  0.00     -
 - Infected        -  0.86  0.14
 - Recovered       -     -  1.00

plot_incidence(sir_builder)








We can even create a plot of the model structure using draw_mermaid():

draw_mermaid(sir_builder, allow_self_transitions = TRUE) |>
  plot()





This is the full model builder pattern in its simplest form: create an empty model, add parameters, add states with their update rules, attach a virus, add a population, and run.

title: Building models with the model builder
text:
Extending the model to SEIRD
We can now use the same pattern to build a model with five states:

Susceptible
Exposed
Infected
Recovered
Deceased

The main new feature is that infected agents can transition to more than one state. In this example, they can recover or die.

seird_builder <- Model() |>
  add_param("Transmission rate", 0.01) |>
  add_param("Incubation rate", 1 / 5) |>
  add_param("Recovery rate", 1 / 7) |>
  add_param("Death rate", 0.01)

The exposed state has one possible transition, from exposed to infected. The infected state has two possible transitions, one to recovery and one to death. These are specified by passing a vector of parameter names and a matching vector of target states. Since this model has an incubation period (Exposed state), we also need to exclude the exposed and recovered states from the susceptible update function. The exclusion only applies to agents carrying the virus:

# We exclude exposed from the sampling
update_s <- update_fun_susceptible(exclude = c(3L, 4L))
update_e <- update_fun_rate(
  param_names = "Incubation rate",
  target_states = 2L
)
update_i <- update_fun_rate(
  param_names = c("Recovery rate", "Death rate"),
  target_states = c(3L, 4L)
)

Now we add the five states:

seird_builder |>
  add_state("Susceptible", update_s) |>
  add_state("Exposed", update_e) |>
  add_state("Infected", update_i) |>
  add_state("Recovered", NULL) |>
  add_state("Deceased", NULL)

data.frame(
  index = 0:(length(get_states(seird_builder)) - 1),
  state = get_states(seird_builder)
)

  index       state
1     0 Susceptible
2     1     Exposed
3     2    Infected
4     3   Recovered
5     4    Deceased


As before, the order is what determines the state indices. In particular:

1L is "Exposed",
3L is "Recovered",
4L is "Deceased".

Those indices are used when defining the virus. Newly infected agents enter the exposed state, recovered agents end in the recovered state, and agents removed through death end in the deceased state.

virus(
  name = "COVID-19",
  prevalence = 0.01,
  as_proportion = TRUE,
  prob_infecting = 0.01,
  recovery_rate = 1 / 7,
  prob_death = 0.01
) |>
  virus_set_state(init = 1L, end = 3L, removed = 4L) |>
  set_prob_infecting_ptr(seird_builder, "Transmission rate") |>
  set_distribution_virus(distribute_virus_randomly(0.01, TRUE)) |>
  # Notice we can also use the pipe here!
  add_virus(model = seird_builder)

The remainder is the same as before: define the population, run the model, and inspect the output.

agents_smallworld(seird_builder, n = 5000, k = 10, d = FALSE, p = 0.01)
verbose_off(seird_builder)

run(seird_builder, ndays = 100, seed = 1912)
summary(seird_builder)

________________________________________________________________________________
________________________________________________________________________________
SIMULATION STUDY

Name of the model   : (none)
Population size     : 5000
Agents' data        : (none)
Number of entities  : 0
Days (duration)     : 100 (of 100)
Number of viruses   : 1
Last run elapsed t  : 6.00ms
Last run speed      : 77.65 million agents x day / second
Rewiring            : off
Last seed used      : 1912

Global events:
 (none)

Virus(es):
 - COVID-19

Tool(s):
 (none)

Model parameters:
 - Death rate        : 0.0100
 - Incubation rate   : 0.2000
 - Recovery rate     : 0.1429
 - Transmission rate : 0.0100

Distribution of the population at time 100:
  - (0) Susceptible : 4950 -> 4766
  - (1) Exposed     :   50 -> 0
  - (2) Infected    :    0 -> 0
  - (3) Recovered   :    0 -> 223
  - (4) Deceased    :    0 -> 11

Transition Probabilities:
 - Susceptible  1.00  0.00     -     -     -
 - Exposed         -  0.81  0.19     -     -
 - Infected        -     -  0.85  0.14  0.01
 - Recovered       -     -     -  1.00     -
 - Deceased        -     -     -     -  1.00

plot_incidence(seird_builder)








We can also draw the transition matrix:

draw_mermaid(seird_builder, allow_self_transitions = TRUE) |>
  plot()





This reproduces the main SEIRD compartment logic using the model builder functions. The key step is the infected-state update:

update_i

<pointer: 0x55555c359580>
attr(,"class")
[1] "epiworld_update_fun"


That single object encodes two possible exits from infection. Once that pattern is clear, building other compartment models becomes much easier: add the states you need, then give each state an update rule that matches the transitions you want to allow.

title: Building models with the model builder
text:
A note about calibration
Calibrating ABMs is not straightforward, especially in the case of network-based models (see for instance Huang et al. (2025)). Nonetheless, we can still have a nice starting point anchored in theory. One way to approach this is using the basic reproductive number ℛ0\mathcal{R}_0; based on how we parametrize models in epiworld, we can write:
ℛ0=c×pt×pr−1⟹c=ℛ0×pr×pt−1
\mathcal{R}_0 = c\times p_t \times p_r^{-1} \implies c = \mathcal{R}_0 \times p_r \times p_t^{-1}

Where cc is the contact rate, ptp_t is the transmission probability, and prp_r is the recovery probability. So, in the case of our last model, we could say the following: given the transmission rate is 0.01 and recovery is 1/7, if we use an ℛ0=2\mathcal{R}_0 = 2, then the contact rate should be:

r_0 <- 2
p_t <- 0.01
p_r <- 1/7

(c_rate <- r_0 * p_r / p_t)

[1] 28.57143


which, in our network based model, means setting the mean degree to be approximately 28.57. As a practical recommendation, I would suggest the user to set a higher contact/degree rate and modify the transmission rate if possible (which would require re-arranging the ℛ0\mathcal{R}_0 equation); otherwise, as the contact rate reduces, the local competition and local depletion of susceptibles effects described in Huang et al. (2025) become apparent. As the contact rate increases, both mixing and network-based models start behaving as compartmental models, which may be desirable in many cases.

title: Part 2: Network Models
text: In this example, we will create a simple SEIR model using a Stochastic-Block Model for generating a random network (included in epiworldR). This creates a network with, in this case, two groups of 500 agents each, with a higher contact rate within groups than between groups.

title: Part 2: Network Models
text:
Exercises

Basic model. Look for the ModelSEIRCONN() and create an Agent-Based model featuring 10,000 agents. Run a simulation with the following characteristics: (a) has a virus with 1 initial case, (b) has a transmission rate of 0.1 with a reproductive number of 2 (so you need to figure out the contact rate), (c) runs for 50 days.







TipSimple ABM calibration



In the case of a simple ABM (based on a fixed contact and transmission rate), we can use the following equation to calibrate our models:
ℛ0=pt×c/pr⟹c=ℛ0×pr/pt
\mathcal{R}_0 = p_t \times c / p_r \implies c = \mathcal{R}_0 \times p_r / p_t

Where cc is the contact rate, prp_r is the recovery rate, and ptp_t is the transmission rate.



Multiple runs. Look at the documentation of run_multiple() and (a) create a saver that extracts the outbreak size, (b) execute the model for the same 50 days, and (c) extract the saved outbreak size and plot the distribution.
A network model. Using the ModelSEIR() function, (a) create a version of the model using a network instead (you can use the small-world network), (b) replicate the run_multiple() exercise. and (d) compare the distribution with and without network







TipCalibration of Network Models



Calibrating network-based models is not a simple task. The traditional sense of the basic reproductive number is somewhat misleading in network-based models overestimating the quantity (see Huang et al. (2025)). Nonetheless, for the moment, it will still give you a good starting point.
